revision.comment: Created page with "'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) is an archaeological site located in Oka, [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka Village]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], consisting of several stone structures. The Sakafuneishi Ruins include the previously known Sakafuneishi along with the turtle-shaped stone artifact, oval-shaped stone artifact, sandstone retaining walls, rammed earth, and surrounding structures discovered during excavations in 2000 (Heisei 12). This naming was made by..."
revision.text:
'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) is an archaeological site located in Oka, [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka Village]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], consisting of several stone structures.
The Sakafuneishi Ruins include the previously known Sakafuneishi along with the turtle-shaped stone artifact, oval-shaped stone artifact, sandstone retaining walls, rammed earth, and surrounding structures discovered during excavations in 2000 (Heisei 12). This naming was made by the Asuka Village Board of Education, though some researchers question the relationship between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact, suggesting the name may not be entirely appropriate{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3: "Sakafuneishi Ruins - The Emperor's Water Ritual Site"}}.
There has been debate about whether this site was part of the Ryōsokimiya (両槻宮), which was situated on Tamimine (distinct in location and range), or served as an entrance facility for it{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3: "Sakafuneishi Ruins - The Emperor's Water Ritual Site"}}. However, archaeological evidence does not support this connection{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}}]] Sakafuneishi is a granite artifact located on a small hill. Its main axis is nearly east-west, with an existing length of about 5.5 meters, a width (north-south) of approximately 2.3 meters, and a thickness of about 1 meter, though portions to the north and south are missing. The upper surface features dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six circular or elliptical depressions, the central one being elongated, measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. Grooves connect these depressions, with the main groove along the central axis being 10 centimeters wide, 3 centimeters deep, and U-shaped in cross-section.
The purpose of Sakafuneishi has been debated since the Edo period, with theories suggesting it was used for brewing sake, manufacturing medicine, or as part of a garden facility. Its exact function remains unclear<ref>References to Sakafuneishi appear in early works such as Motoori Norinaga's *Sugegasasa Nikki* and Ueda Akinari's *Iwahashi no Ki*.</ref>. Additionally, grooves discovered in 1935 near Sakafuneishi suggest it might have been part of a water feature<ref>Kenekatsu Inokuma proposed that Sakafuneishi and other nearby features were part of a water flow facility similar to those seen in [[Gyeongju]], South Korea.</ref>. Some scholars also hypothesize that it functioned as an ancient {{ill|fluidic device|ja|流体素子}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu|date=2017-12|title=Considerations on Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device|journal=Nagare: Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics|publisher=Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics|volume=36|issue=6|pages=417-420|id={{NDLJP|11453167}}|url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167}}</ref>.
Marks from stone chisels, likely from attempts to reuse the stone for constructing [[Takatori Castle]], are visible, contributing to the damaged state of the artifact's surface.
Sakafuneishi was designated a national historic site on April 8, 1927.
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped stone artifact, oval-shaped stone artifact, and water source facility}}]] During excavations in 1992 (Heisei 4), sandstone walls were discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. These were speculated to correspond to construction described in the *Nihon Shoki* during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}. In 2000 (Heisei 12), large-scale excavations revealed sandstone structures identified as a water source facility, along with the turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts. These artifacts appear to have served as water basins connected to the water source facility.
The turtle-shaped stone artifact, made of granite, is approximately 2.4 meters long and 2 meters wide, with sculpted features representing a head, tail, and legs. The back has a hollowed section forming a basin. Water flowed in through an opening in the head and out through the tail. The oval-shaped stone artifact, measuring 1.65 meters in length and 1 meter in width, also served as a water basin connected to the turtle artifact's head. The exact cultural or ritual significance of these artifacts remains debated.
== Stone Hills and Sandstone Walls == The hill where Sakafuneishi is located has been extensively modified with rammed earth and sandstone blocks from nearby quarries, creating an artificial landscape stretching approximately 700 meters. Sandstone retaining walls and collapsed stones found on the western slope suggest episodes of repair and reconstruction, possibly linked to the seismic activity recorded in the *Nihon Shoki*.
== Izumino Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafune1916 1.JPEG|thumb|240px|{{center|Izumino Sakafuneishi}}]] Located about 400 meters from the Sakafuneishi Ruins, Izumino Sakafuneishi was discovered in 1916 in the Izumino area of Asuka. It shares similar water channels and grooves with Sakafuneishi. Currently, the original artifact resides in a private garden in Kyoto and is not open to public viewing, though a replica is displayed at the [[Asuka Historical Museum]].
== Notable Appearances == The Sakafuneishi replica was featured in the 1983 film *{{ill|Satomi Hakkenden|ja|里見八犬伝 (1983年の映画)}}*.
== Nearby Facilities == * [[Nara Prefectural Complex of Man'yo Culture]] * Asuka Folk Museum
== Access == Approximately 3 km east of [[Kashiharajingu-mae Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Minoru Chida, Takao Uno (editors)|year=2000|title=Ancient Studies on Turtles|publisher=Toho Publishing|isbn=4-88591-707-7}} * {{Cite book|author=Teiji Kadowaki|year=2002|title=Asuka and Turtle-Shaped Stone Artifacts|publisher=Gakuseisha|isbn=4-311-20250-4}} * {{Cite book|author=Kunihiko Kawakami|year=2003|title=Excavating Asuka|series=Kodansha Selection Mechie|publisher=Kodansha|isbn=4-06-258258-9}} * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshiyuki Aihara|title=Excavation of Sakafuneishi Ruins and Its Significance|journal=Journal of Japanese Archaeology|volume=11|issue=18|pages=171-180|date=2004|doi=10.11215/nihonkokogaku1994.11.18_171}}
== Related Articles == * [[Stone Artifacts of Asuka]] * [[Cup-Marked Stones]] * [[Shitennoji Kameido]]
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Asuka Village Official Website]
[[Category:Asuka Period sites]] [[Category:Archaeological sites in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Asuka, Nara]] [[Category:Stone sculptures]] [[ja:酒船石遺跡]]

'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, [[Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in {{ill|Heisei 12|ja|平成12年}} (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰}}—but archaeological evidence does not support this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}}]] This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The upper surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an apparatus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}}'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and {{ill|Ueda Akinari|ja|上田秋成}}'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the {{ill|Kurumaishi|ja|車石}} artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient {{ill|fluidic device|ja|流体素子}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}} stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} of a construction project during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}} involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]
[[Category:Asuka Period ruins]] [[Category:Archaeological sites in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Asuka, Nara]] [[Category:Stone works]] [[ja:酒船石遺跡]]

It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} of a construction project during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}} involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stoneworks|ja|飛鳥の石造物}} * {{ill|Cupules|ja|盃状穴}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the {{ill|Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代}} connected to a similar ritual facility. During the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.

revision.text:
'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, [[Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in {{ill|Heisei 12|ja|平成12年}} (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰}}—but archaeological evidence does not support this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}}]] This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The upper surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an apparatus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}}'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and {{ill|Ueda Akinari|ja|上田秋成}}'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the {{ill|Kurumaishi|ja|車石}} artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient {{ill|fluidic device|ja|流体素子}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}} stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} of a construction project during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}} involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stoneworks|ja|飛鳥の石造物}} * {{ill|Cupules|ja|盃状穴}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the {{ill|Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代}} connected to a similar ritual facility. During the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]

revision.text:
'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, [[Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in {{ill|Heisei 12|ja|平成12年}} (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰}}—but archaeological evidence does not support this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}}]] This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The upper surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an apparatus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}}'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and {{ill|Ueda Akinari|ja|上田秋成}}'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the {{ill|Kurumaishi|ja|車石}} artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient {{ill|fluidic device|ja|流体素子}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}} stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} of a construction project during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}} involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stoneworks|ja|飛鳥の石造物}} * {{ill|Cupules|ja|盃状穴}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the {{ill|Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代}} connected to a similar ritual facility. During the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]

Based on its proximity to the discovery of the {{ill|Kurumaishi|ja|車石}} artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient {{ill|fluidic device|ja|流体素子}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}} stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} of a construction project during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}} involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stoneworks|ja|飛鳥の石造物}} * {{ill|Cupules|ja|盃状穴}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the {{ill|Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代}} connected to a similar ritual facility. During the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]

revision.text:
'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, [[Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in {{ill|Heisei 12|ja|平成12年}} (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰}}—but archaeological evidence does not support this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}}]] This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The upper surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an apparatus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}}'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and {{ill|Ueda Akinari|ja|上田秋成}}'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the {{ill|Kurumaishi|ja|車石}} artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient {{ill|fluidic device|ja|流体素子}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}} stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} of a construction project during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}} involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stoneworks|ja|飛鳥の石造物}} * {{ill|Cupules|ja|盃状穴}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the {{ill|Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代}} connected to a similar ritual facility. During the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]

revision.text:
'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, [[Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in {{ill|Heisei 12|ja|平成12年}} (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰}}—but archaeological evidence does not support this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}}]] This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The upper surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an apparatus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}}'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and {{ill|Ueda Akinari|ja|上田秋成}}'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the {{ill|Kurumaishi|ja|車石}} artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient {{ill|fluidic device|ja|流体素子}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}} stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} of a construction project during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}} involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stoneworks|ja|飛鳥の石造物}} * {{ill|Cupules|ja|盃状穴}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the {{ill|Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代}} connected to a similar ritual facility. During the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]

Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The upper surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an apparatus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}}'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and {{ill|Ueda Akinari|ja|上田秋成}}'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the {{ill|Kurumaishi|ja|車石}} artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient {{ill|fluidic device|ja|流体素子}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}} stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} of a construction project during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}} involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stoneworks|ja|飛鳥の石造物}} * {{ill|Cupules|ja|盃状穴}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the {{ill|Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代}} connected to a similar ritual facility. During the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]

There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient {{ill|fluidic device|ja|流体素子}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}} stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}} [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} of a construction project during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}} involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stoneworks|ja|飛鳥の石造物}} * {{ill|Cupules|ja|盃状穴}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the {{ill|Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代}} connected to a similar ritual facility. During the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.

revision.text:
'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, [[Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in {{ill|Heisei 12|ja|平成12年}} (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰}}—but archaeological evidence does not support this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}} This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The upper surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an apparatus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}}'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and {{ill|Ueda Akinari|ja|上田秋成}}'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient {{ill|fluidic device|ja|流体素子}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.

In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}} stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}} [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} of a construction project during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}} involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stoneworks|ja|飛鳥の石造物}} * {{ill|Cupules|ja|盃状穴}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the {{ill|Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代}} connected to a similar ritual facility. During the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.

revision.text:
'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, [[Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in {{ill|Heisei 12|ja|平成12年}} (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰}}—but archaeological evidence does not support this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}} This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The upper surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an apparatus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}}'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and {{ill|Ueda Akinari|ja|上田秋成}}'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the {{ill|Kurumaishi|ja|車石}} artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient {{ill|fluidic device|ja|流体素子}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}} stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}} [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} of a construction project during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}} involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stoneworks|ja|飛鳥の石造物}} * {{ill|Cupules|ja|盃状穴}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the {{ill|Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代}} connected to a similar ritual facility. During the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]

There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient {{ill|fluidic device|ja|流体素子}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}} stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}} [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} of a construction project during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}} involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stoneworks|ja|飛鳥の石造物}} * {{ill|Cupules|ja|盃状穴}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the {{ill|Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代}} connected to a similar ritual facility. During the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]

revision.text:
'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, [[Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in {{ill|Heisei 12|ja|平成12年}} (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰}}—but archaeological evidence does not support this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}} This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The upper surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an apparatus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}}'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and {{ill|Ueda Akinari|ja|上田秋成}}'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the {{ill|Kurumaishi|ja|車石}} artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient {{ill|fluidic device|ja|流体素子}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}} stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}} [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} of a construction project during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}} involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stoneworks|ja|飛鳥の石造物}} * {{ill|Cupules|ja|盃状穴}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the {{ill|Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代}} connected to a similar ritual facility. During the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.

Based on its proximity to the discovery of the {{ill|Kurumaishi|ja|車石}} artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient {{ill|fluidic device|ja|流体素子}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}} stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}} [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} of a construction project during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}} involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stoneworks|ja|飛鳥の石造物}} * {{ill|Cupules|ja|盃状穴}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the {{ill|Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代}} connected to a similar ritual facility. During the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.

revision.text:
'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, [[Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in {{ill|Heisei 12|ja|平成12年}} (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰}}—but archaeological evidence does not support this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}} This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The upper surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an apparatus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}}'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and {{ill|Ueda Akinari|ja|上田秋成}}'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the {{ill|Kurumaishi|ja|車石}} artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient {{ill|fluidic device|ja|流体素子}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}} stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}} [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} of a construction project during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}} involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stoneworks|ja|飛鳥の石造物}} * {{ill|Cupules|ja|盃状穴}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the {{ill|Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代}} connected to a similar ritual facility. During the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]
