revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Asuka Stoneworks]] → [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
revision.text:
'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka, Nara|qq=}}, [[Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in {{ill|Heisei 12|ja|平成12nd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 12|qq=}} (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|qq=}}—but archaeological evidence does not su11ort this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}}]] This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The u11er surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an a11aratus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Motoori Norinaga|qq=}}'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and {{ill|Ueda Akinari|ja|上田秋成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Akinari|qq=}}'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the {{ill|Kurumaishi|ja|車石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurumaishi|qq=}} artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inokuma Kanemasa|qq=}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient {{ill|fluidic device|ja|流体素子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fluidic device|qq=}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatori Castle|qq=}} stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni no Manaita|qq=}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 4|qq=}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} of a construction project during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Saimei|qq=}} involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == See Also == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stoneworks|qq=}} * {{ill|Cupules|ja|盃状穴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cupules|qq=}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|qq=}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the {{ill|Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Period|qq=}} connected to a similar ritual facility. During the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura Period|qq=}}, the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]
{{translated page|ja|酒船石遺跡|version=103912080|comment=Imported full ja history}}

'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka, Nara|qq=}}, [[Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in {{ill|Heisei 12|ja|平成12nd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 12|qq=}} (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|qq=}}—but archaeological evidence does not su11ort this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}}]] This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The u11er surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an a11aratus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Motoori Norinaga|qq=}}'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and {{ill|Ueda Akinari|ja|上田秋成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Akinari|qq=}}'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the {{ill|Kurumaishi|ja|車石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurumaishi|qq=}} artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inokuma Kanemasa|qq=}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient {{ill|fluidic device|ja|流体素子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fluidic device|qq=}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatori Castle|qq=}} stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni no Manaita|qq=}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 4|qq=}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} of a construction project during the reign of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Saimei|qq=}} involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == See Also == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stoneworks|qq=}} * {{ill|Cupules|ja|盃状穴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cupules|qq=}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|qq=}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the {{ill|Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Period|qq=}} connected to a similar ritual facility. During the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura Period|qq=}}, the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]
{{translated page|ja|酒船石遺跡|version=103912080|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Theories about its purpose have ranged from an a11aratus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>[[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]]'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and [[:en:Ueda Akinari|Ueda Akinari]]'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the [[:en:Stone routes|Kurumaishi]] artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inokuma Kanemasa|qq=}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient [[:en:Fluidics|fluidic device]]<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]] stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni no Manaita|qq=}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 4|qq=}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] of a construction project during the reign of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]] involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == See Also == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stoneworks|qq=}} * [[:en:Cup and ring mark|Cupules]] * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|qq=}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka Period]] connected to a similar ritual facility. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura Period]], the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:奈良県にある国指定の史跡
revision.text:
'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in {{ill|Heisei 12|ja|平成12年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 12|qq=}} (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|qq=}}—but archaeological evidence does not su11ort this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}}]] This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The u11er surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an a11aratus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>[[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]]'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and [[:en:Ueda Akinari|Ueda Akinari]]'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the [[:en:Stone routes|Kurumaishi]] artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inokuma Kanemasa|qq=}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient [[:en:Fluidics|fluidic device]]<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]] stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni no Manaita|qq=}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 4|qq=}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] of a construction project during the reign of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]] involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == See Also == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stoneworks|qq=}} * [[:en:Cup and ring mark|Cupules]] * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|qq=}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka Period]] connected to a similar ritual facility. In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in {{ill|Heisei 12|ja|平成12年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 12|qq=}} (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|qq=}}—but archaeological evidence does not su11ort this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}}]] This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The u11er surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an a11aratus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>[[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]]'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and [[:en:Ueda Akinari|Ueda Akinari]]'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the [[:en:Stone routes|Kurumaishi]] artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inokuma Kanemasa|qq=}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient [[:en:Fluidics|fluidic device]]<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]] stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni no Manaita|qq=}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 4|qq=}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] of a construction project during the reign of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]] involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == See Also == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stoneworks|qq=}} * [[:en:Cup and ring mark|Cupules]] * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|qq=}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka Period]] connected to a similar ritual facility. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura Period]], the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]

During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura Period]], the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.

revision.comment: Bot: update category link → National historic sites in Nara prefecture
revision.text:
'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in {{ill|Heisei 12|ja|平成12年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 12|qq=}} (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|qq=}}—but archaeological evidence does not su11ort this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}}]] This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The u11er surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an a11aratus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>[[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]]'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and [[:en:Ueda Akinari|Ueda Akinari]]'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the [[:en:Stone routes|Kurumaishi]] artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inokuma Kanemasa|qq=}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient [[:en:Fluidics|fluidic device]]<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]] stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni no Manaita|qq=}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 4|qq=}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] of a construction project during the reign of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]] involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == See Also == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stoneworks|qq=}} * [[:en:Cup and ring mark|Cupules]] * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|qq=}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka Period]] connected to a similar ritual facility. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura Period]], the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]

== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 4|qq=}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] of a construction project during the reign of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]] involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == See Also == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stoneworks|qq=}} * [[:en:Cup and ring mark|Cupules]] * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|qq=}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka Period]] connected to a similar ritual facility. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura Period]], the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Archaeological sites in Nara Prefecture
revision.text:
'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in {{ill|Heisei 12|ja|平成12年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 12|qq=}} (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|qq=}}—but archaeological evidence does not su11ort this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}}]] This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The u11er surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an a11aratus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>[[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]]'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and [[:en:Ueda Akinari|Ueda Akinari]]'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the [[:en:Stone routes|Kurumaishi]] artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inokuma Kanemasa|qq=}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient [[:en:Fluidics|fluidic device]]<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]] stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni no Manaita|qq=}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja|平成4年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 4|qq=}} (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] of a construction project during the reign of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]] involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == See Also == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stoneworks|qq=}} * [[:en:Cup and ring mark|Cupules]] * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|qq=}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka Period]] connected to a similar ritual facility. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura Period]], the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]

Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The u11er surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an a11aratus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>[[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]]'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and [[:en:Ueda Akinari|Ueda Akinari]]'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the [[:en:Stone routes|Kurumaishi]] artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inokuma Kanemasa|qq=}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient [[:en:Fluidics|fluidic device]]<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]] stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni no Manaita|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In {{ill|Heisei 4|ja||1992年|1=1992|2=ja|3=1992年|lt=Heisei 4|lt_ja=平成4年||qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 4|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1992年}}[[Category:Years needing redirect replacements]] (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] of a construction project during the reign of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]] involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == See Also == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stoneworks|qq=}} * [[:en:Cup and ring mark|Cupules]] * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|qq=}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka Period]] connected to a similar ritual facility. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura Period]], the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]

revision.text:
'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in [[:en:2000|Heisei 12]][[Category:Years needing redirect replacements]] (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|qq=}}—but archaeological evidence does not su11ort this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}}]] This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The u11er surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an a11aratus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>[[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]]'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and [[:en:Ueda Akinari|Ueda Akinari]]'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the [[:en:Stone routes|Kurumaishi]] artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inokuma Kanemasa|qq=}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient [[:en:Fluidics|fluidic device]]<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]] stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni no Manaita|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In [[:en:1992|Heisei 4]][[Category:Years needing redirect replacements]] (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] of a construction project during the reign of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]] involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == See Also == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stoneworks|qq=}} * [[:en:Cup and ring mark|Cupules]] * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|qq=}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka Period]] connected to a similar ritual facility. revision.text:
'''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in [[:en:2000|Heisei 12]] (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|qq=}}—but archaeological evidence does not su11ort this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}}]] This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The u11er surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an a11aratus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>[[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]]'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and [[:en:Ueda Akinari|Ueda Akinari]]'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the [[:en:Stone routes|Kurumaishi]] artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inokuma Kanemasa|qq=}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient [[:en:Fluidics|fluidic device]]<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]] stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni no Manaita|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In [[:en:1992|Heisei 4]] (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] of a construction project during the reign of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]] involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == See Also == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stoneworks|qq=}} * [[:en:Cup and ring mark|Cupules]] * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|qq=}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka Period]] connected to a similar ritual facility. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura Period]], the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]
{{translated page|ja|酒船石遺跡|version=103912080|comment=Imported full ja history}}

It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In [[:en:1992|Heisei 4]] (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == See Also == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stoneworks|qq=}} * [[:en:Cup and ring mark|Cupules]] * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|qq=}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka Period]] connected to a similar ritual facility. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura Period]], the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]

<!--Sakafuneishi Site--><!--Sakafuneishi Site--><!--Sakafuneishi Site--><!--Sakafuneishi Site--><!--Sakafuneishi Site--><!--Sakafuneishi Site--> [[Category:National historic sites in Nara prefecture]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove incorrect Shona (sn:) interwiki links
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11644368]] Wikidata name: Sakafuneishi Site [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in [[:en:2000|Heisei 12]] (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|qq=|1=Mount Tōnomine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430808}}—but archaeological evidence does not su11ort this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}}]] This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill. Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The u11er surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an a11aratus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>[[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]]'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and [[:en:Ueda Akinari|Ueda Akinari]]'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the [[:en:Stone routes|Kurumaishi]] artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inokuma Kanemasa|qq=|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient [[:en:Fluidics|fluidic device]]<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]] stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni no Manaita|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠|1=Oni no Manaita and Oni no Setchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673378}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In [[:en:1992|Heisei 4]] (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] of a construction project during the reign of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]] involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == See Also == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stoneworks|qq=}} * [[:en:Cup and ring mark|Cupules]] * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|qq=}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka Period]] connected to a similar ritual facility. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura Period]], the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]

There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|qq=|1=Mount Tōnomine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430808}}—but archaeological evidence does not su11ort this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.

Its main axis is oriented east-west, with current dimensions of approximately 5.5 meters in length, 2.3 meters in width (north-south), and 1 meter in thickness, though parts of the north and south ends are missing. The u11er surface features several dish-shaped depressions connected by grooves. There are six depressions, with the central one being an elongated oval measuring 1.35 meters in length and 70 centimeters in width. The grooves connecting the depressions are about 10 centimeters wide and 3 centimeters deep, with a U-shaped cross-section.
Theories about its purpose have ranged from an a11aratus for brewing sake to a facility for producing medicines, but its exact use remains uncertain<ref>[[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]]'s "Sugagasa Nikki" mentions "a sake tub of an ancient nobleman," and [[:en:Ueda Akinari|Ueda Akinari]]'s "Iwabashi no Ki" refers to it as "the renowned sake tub."</ref>. Based on its proximity to the discovery of the [[:en:Stone routes|Kurumaishi]] artifact—believed to be part of a garden structure—some speculate Sakafuneishi was part of a water-flowing garden feature<ref>{{ill|Inokuma Kanemasa|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inokuma Kanemasa|qq=|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} proposed this theory in "Sakafuneishi," *Asuka Breeze*, 1982.</ref>. There is also a hypothesis that it served as an ancient [[:en:Fluidics|fluidic device]]<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Murakami Yui, Kubota Yoshihiro, Mochizuki Osamu |title=Study on the Possibility of Sakafuneishi as a Fluidic Device |journal=NAGARE : Journal of the Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |date=2017-12 |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=417-420 |publisher=Tokyo: Japan Society of Fluid Mechanics |id={{NDLJP|11453167}} |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I11453167 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}</ref>.
In later periods, including the Edo era, Sakafuneishi was damaged by attempts to use it as material for constructing [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]] stone walls. Tool marks for splitting the stone remain visible{{Efn|Similar damage is seen on {{ill|Oni no Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni no Manaita|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠|1=Oni no Manaita and Oni no Setchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673378}}.}}.
It was designated as a National Historic Site on April 8, 1927 (Showa 2).
== Turtle-Shaped and Oval-Shaped Stone Artifacts and Ritual Site == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Kamegata Sekizoubutsu.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Turtle-shaped and oval-shaped stone artifacts with a springwater facility.}}]] [[File:Sakafune-ishi remains 20000227.jpg|thumb|240px|Public viewing of the Sakafuneishi Ruins on February 27, 2000]] In [[:en:1992|Heisei 4]] (1992), a stone embankment was discovered on the northern slope of Sakafuneishi. This structure is believed to correspond to descriptions in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] of a construction project during the reign of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Saimei]] involving "piling stones to form a fence on the eastern mountain of the palace"{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=171}}.
Subsequent excavations in 2000 uncovered a springwater facility, oval-shaped stone artifact, and turtle-shaped stone artifact. These artifacts, functioning as water tanks, are thought to have collected water from the spring. The springwater facility is a rectangular structure made of sandstone blocks stacked in 11 tiers with an internal hollow, featuring a concave intake on the top north side and surrounded by a sandstone embankment{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=175}}.
The turtle-shaped artifact is approximately 2.4 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and features a carved shell with a bowl-like hollow. The oval-shaped artifact measures 1.65 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and also has a bowl-shaped hollow connected to the turtle's "head." These artifacts may have served ceremonial purposes for imperial water rituals. == See Also == {{Commonscat|Sakafuneishi Ruins}} * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stoneworks|qq=}} * [[:en:Cup and ring mark|Cupules]] * {{ill|Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|ja|四天王寺#伽藍|Shitennō-ji Kameido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitennō-ji#Temple Grounds|qq=}} - The Kameido at Shitennō-ji contains a water tank from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka Period]] connected to a similar ritual facility. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura Period]], the turtle-shaped tank was altered and reversed, revealing a style resembling the Sakafuneishi Ruins, suggesting its use for water rituals.
== External Links == * [http://www.asukamura.jp/ Official Asuka Village Website]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sakafuneishi Ruins]][[ja:酒船石遺跡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11644368]] Wikidata name: Sakafuneishi Site [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakafuneishi Ruins''' (酒船石遺跡) are a collection of stone artifacts located in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]].
In addition to the previously known '''Sakafuneishi''' (酒船石), the discovery of a turtle-shaped stone artifact and an oval-shaped stone artifact, along with sandstone embankments and tamped earth structures during excavations in [[:en:2000|Heisei 12]] (2000), led to the designation of the entire area as the Sakafuneishi Ruins. This naming, by the Asuka Village Board of Education, has been questioned by some researchers who doubt the connection between Sakafuneishi and the turtle-shaped stone artifact{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}.
There is debate about whether the ruins were part of the Ryōtsukimiya Palace or its entrance facility, located in Tami Peak (田身嶺)—distinct from the location and range of {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|1=Mount Tōnomine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430808}}—but archaeological evidence does not su11ort this hypothesis{{Sfn|Kawakami|2003|loc=Chapter 3, "Sakafuneishi Ruins—Imperial Water Ritual Site"}}{{Sfn|Aihara|2004|p=177}}.
== Sakafuneishi == [[File:Sakafuneishi Ruins, Sakafuneishi-1.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Sakafuneishi}}]] This artifact is a granite stone structure located on a small hill.
